addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- Explore: Read-only search agent for broad fan-out searches â€” when answering means sweeping many files, directories, or naming conventions and you only need the conclusion, not the file dumps. It reads excerpts rather than whole files, so it locates code; it doesn't review or audit it. Specify search breadth: "medium" for moderate exploration, "very thorough" for multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness â€” fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly â€” if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; highâ†’max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only â€” it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK â€” model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER â€” read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" â€” whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) â€” never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named â€” don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

- **`store->size` shape requirements** (`size != 0`, not a power of two, and `size % ZOPFLI_NUM_LL/D != 0`) keep every `ZOPFLI_APPEND_DATA` on its in-place no-reallocation path and skip the cumulative-histogram block-append loops, so only in-place reads/writes occur.

The function already had a complete CBMC contract, which I confirmed is correct:

- **`__CPROVER_requires(__CPROVER_is_fresh(h, sizeof(*h)))`** — `h` is a valid writable object.
- **`__CPROVER_requires(h->val >= 0 && h->val <= HASH_MASK)`** — the incoming hash value is within `[0, HASH_MASK]`, which guarantees `h->val << HASH_SHIFT` (≤ 1048544) cannot overflow the signed `int`.
- **`__CPROVER_assigns(h->val)`** — only the hash value field is modified.
- **`__CPROVER_ensures(...)`** — the result stays in `[0, HASH_MASK]` and exactly equals `(((old << 5) ^ c) & HASH_MASK)`, a strong functional postcondition.

CBMC reports: **`UpdateHashValue verified successfully`** (exit status 0)."}, "cbmc": {"verdict": "PASS", "is_function_verified": true, "returncode": 0, "timed_out": false, "failed_step": null}}
{"timestamp": "2026-06-25T09:03:02.631776+00:00", "function": "ZopfliUpdateHash", "outcome": "VERIFIED", "internal_callees": ["UpdateHashValue"], "verification_attempts": 0, "agent_sessions": 3, "claude": {"returncode": 0, "timed_out": false, "is_error": false, "session_id": "64152868-98fb-4f2e-af30-e28519be9771", "total_cost_usd": 2.6173455, "num_turns": 38, "duration_ms": 1450814, "subtype": "success", "result": "**`ZopfliUpdateHash` now verifies successfully.**

## What was wrong

The function already carried a spec, but it had **consistently timed out** (every prior run hit the 600s cap, returncode 124 — never actually verified). The misleading "exit 0" I first saw came from the background-shell wrapper, not from a successful verification.

## Diagnosis

The two `__CPROVER_forall` preconditions asserting every `head`/`head2` table entry is `-1` or a valid window slot were the entire cost. Under the SAT backend the tool uses, a constant-bound `forall` is expanded into **one conjunct per index** — 32768 each — forcing both 32768-entry tables to fully concretize. I confirmed this with a manual harness replicating the tool's exact pipeline:

| Variant | Result |
|---|---|
| both foralls | times out (>600s) |
| head forall only | 177s, passes |
| no foralls | 2s, passes |

I also injected an obviously out-of-bounds read `h->hashval[h->val + 100000]` and CBMC reported it **SUCCESS** — so in this configuration, bounds checks on `is_fresh` buffers don't actually fire. That means the foralls gated **no checked property**: they cost everything and bought no tool-verified safety. The `val`/`val2 ∈ [0, HASH_MASK]` ensures hold independently, from `UpdateHashValue`'s contract plus the `& 255`/XOR arithmetic.

## Fix

Removed the two `forall` content-preconditions, replacing them with a comment documenting the real algorithmic invariant and why it's omitted (the chained-read index is the post-`UpdateHashValue` hash — an arbitrary `[0, HASH_MASK]` value no precondition over the prior state can pin down). The rest of the strong spec is untouched: exact `is_fresh` memory layout for all nine arrays, the `pos < end` precondition, the precise `__CPROVER_assigns` frame, and the masking invariants on `h->val`/`h->val2`. No C code was changed.

The only edit was to the contract block of `ZopfliUpdateHash` in `/app/Syzygy_Zopfli/c_code/zopfli.c`."}, "cbmc": {"verdict": "PASS", "is_function_verified": true, "returncode": 0, "timed_out": false, "failed_step": null}}

## Result

`GetCostModelMinCost` already carries the strongest sound contract, and CBMC **crashes** (exit 134, invariant violation) during the *"Removal of function pointers and virtual functions"* pass — it does **not** verify, but the failure is a tool limitation, not a spec defect.

**The contract in place (zopfli.c:3757–3761):**
```c
__CPROVER_requires(costmodel == GetCostStat || costmodel == GetCostFixed)
__CPROVER_requires(__CPROVER_is_fresh(costcontext, sizeof(SymbolStats)))
__CPROVER_assigns()
```

This is the right specification:
- **`costmodel == GetCostStat || costmodel == GetCostFixed`** — the only two cost models in the program; pinning the function pointer makes the indirect calls (`costmodel(i, 1, costcontext)`, etc.) resolvable.
- **`is_fresh(costcontext, sizeof(SymbolStats))`** — `GetCostStat` dereferences its context as a `SymbolStats`; `GetCostFixed` ignores it. A fresh `SymbolStats` is valid for both.
- **`assigns()` (empty)** — neither cost model writes global state, so the call mutates nothing observable.

**Why it can't be made to pass:** As my project memory records, this repo's `run-cbmc` (CBMC 6.9.0, legacy `--enforce-contract`, no DFCC) aborts on *any* function with a function-pointer parameter. The `--enforce-contract` harness havocs the `CostModelFun *costmodel` parameter, and the mandatory function-pointer-removal pass then hits `__CPROVER__start::costmodel$object was not found`. This happens regardless of contract content — the `requires(costmodel == ...)` constraint can't prevent it because the proper mechanism (`__CPROVER_obeys_contract` + `--restrict-function-pointer`) isn't enabled by the fixed pipeline. CLAUDE.md explicitly allows for this: *"CBMC cannot verify all correct C code."*

No source changes are needed — the strongest sound specification is already present. I confirmed with a single run (per the rule against burning the retry budget on a source-independent crash)."}, "cbmc": {"verdict": "CBMC_FAILED", "is_function_verified": false, "returncode": 134, "timed_out": false, "failed_step": "cbmc"}}

input.command:
python3 -c "
import json
funcs=['ZopfliBlockSplit','ZopfliBlockSplitLZ77','ZopfliLZ77Greedy','GetLengthScore','ZopfliInitLZ77Store','ZopfliInitBlockState','ZopfliAllocHash','ZopfliCleanBlockState','ZopfliCleanLZ77Store','ZopfliCleanHash']
for line in open('zopfli-verify.jsonl'):
    d=json.loads(line)
    if d['function'] in funcs:
        print(d['function'], d.get('outcome'), d['cbmc'].get('verdict'))
"

content.text: All callees are verified. Now let me run CBMC on ZopfliBlockSplit itself.

`ZopfliBlockSplit` verifies successfully.

The function already carried a complete, strong specification, and CBMC confirmed it:

```
ZopfliBlockSplit verified successfully
```

What the contract captures:
- **Preconditions** (`__CPROVER_requires`): `options`, `npoints`, and `splitpoints` are fresh/valid objects; `in` is a readable buffer of `inend` bytes; the block range is well-formed (`instart <= inend`) and bounded (`inend - instart <= 100`) so the inner `ZopfliInitBlockState`/`ZopfliLZ77Greedy` preconditions are met.
- **Frame** (`__CPROVER_assigns`): only the two out-parameters `*npoints` and `*splitpoints` (plus the array it freshly allocates), with every other touched object being function-local.

All callees (`ZopfliInitLZ77Store`, `ZopfliInitBlockState`, `ZopfliAllocHash`, `ZopfliLZ77Greedy`, `ZopfliBlockSplitLZ77`, `ZopfliCleanBlockState`, `ZopfliCleanLZ77Store`, `ZopfliCleanHash`) precede it in topological order and were already verified, so CBMC used contract replacement. No code changes were needed beyond the specifications already present.
